course_description: Basic statistical concepts & techniques such as descriptive statistics, probability concepts, theoretical distributions, inferential statistics (confidence intervals & hypothesis testing) are applied in business & economics.

requirements.prerequisites: Maths B or Maths C or MATH1040
requirements.incompatible: CHEE2010 or 3010 or CIVL2530 or MINE3214 or STAT1201 or 1301 or 2201
requirements.assumed_background: Algebra from high school Maths B.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Students are required to sign-on to a lecture and tutorial group via mySI-net.

• Lecture Sign On : (select one option only, either face to face or online studio produced video lectures). Opens at 9:00am Friday 5 July; no closing date as sign on is open the whole semester.

• Tutorial Sign On : Opens at 9:00pm Thursday 11 July; no closing date as sign on is open the whole semester. Enrol in a tutorial group prior to Week 2 and attend the same tutorial group each week.

• PASS Sign On : There is NO need to sign on to attend PASS. The weekly PASS timetable will be posted in teaching week 1 under the "Course Help" tab of the ECON1310 blackboard site.

• Consultation Sign On : There is NO need to sign on to attend consultation. The weekly consultation timetable will be posted in teaching week 1 under the "Course Help" tab of the ECON1310 blackboard site.

Tutorials, Consultations and PASS commence in Teaching Week 2.

Students should refer to the timetable on mySI-net prior to the commencement of classes to ensure that they have the most up to date information, as from time to time late room changes may occur.

Please see the Learning Activities section of this Course Profile (Section 4.1) for the timetabling implications of public holidays.

Public Holidays: Wed 14 August (EKKA), Mon 7 October (Queen's Birthday)

Mid-Semester Break: 30 September - 4 October . Classes will resume on 8 October.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of the course is for students to develop an ability to apply inferential statistics techniques to independently solve practical problems and to then explain the solutions using everyday language.

learning_outcomes.description: recognise, interpret and use fundamental statistical terminology in practice;
apply theoretical concepts to construct analytical statistical techniques;
describe the statistical techniques needed to solve particular problem types;
conduct statistical analysis of data so as to draw statistical conclusions;
communicate statistical findings for practical and professional use.

Saturday 31 August(week 6)

Saturday 7 September (week 7)

Saturday 14 September (week 8)

assessment_details.title: My Own Statistics Story Book (MOSS Book)
assessment_details.task_description:
MOSS Book stands for My Own Statistic Story Book. MOSS Book aids students’ learning of abstract statistical concepts. The activity requires students to create and write short stories that explain key lecture concepts presented in lectures 5 and 6 in non-technical terms.

More detailed information about preparation of the MOSS Book, the marking criteria and the submission requirements are available in the Assessment folder on Blackboard.

Note: MOSS Book is an individual asessment item.

learning_resources.required_resources:
Students do not need to purchase any textbook for this course.

The contents of the textbook written by Black et al. (2019), Business Analytics and Statistics, 1st Edition, Wiley Australia will be made available free via the Blackboard site. It is this textbook that the lectures and tutorials refer to during the course. Hard copies of the textbook are available for purchase from bookshops should students wish to do so.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/ECON1310 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:

Calculator requirements may differ for other university courses and students should check each course profile for the specific requirements.

PLEASE NOTE: It is the student’s responsibility to ensure they have the correct calculator prior to assessment in each of their courses. Students without the correct calculator in a School of Economics exam may have their calculator confiscated.

Guidelines for correct referencing techniques can be found in the School of Economics publication Guide for Assignment Presentation , which can be accessed on the web site www.uq.edu.au/economics

learning_activities.topic: Mon - Fri Lecture 1: Descriptive Statistics 1 : Statistical concepts and definitions; data types, sources, levels; descriptive & inferential statistics; sampling methods and errors. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 1, Ch 7.1 - 7.3)

Mon - Fri Lecture 2: Descriptive Statistics 2 : Measures of central tendency, variation and shape for ungrouped data; box and whisker plot; linear combination of random variables; coefficient of correlation. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 3)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 1: Descriptive Statistics 1 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 1. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 1, Ch 7.1-7.3)
Mon - Fri Lecture 3: Probability 1 : Basic probability concepts, conditional probability. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 4)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 2: Descriptive Statistics 2 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 2. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 3)
Mon - Fri Lecture 4: Probability 2 : Discrete probability distributions, binomial probability. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 5.1-5.3)

Mon - Fri Tutorial 3: Probability 1 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 3. Wednesday, 14 August, is a Public Holiday. Therefore no tutorials, consultations or PASS sessions will be held on that day. Students who normally attend a Wednesday tutorial session are welcome to attend an alternative tutorial session for this week only.

Mon - Fri Lecture 5: Normal Distribution : Understanding the basis of the normal distribution, interpreting the Standardised Normal Distribution table, computing Z - scores and finding probabilities. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 6.1-6.3)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 4: Probability 2 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 4. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 5.1-5.3)
Mon - Fri Lecture 6: Sampling Distributions : Sampling distributions of the mean and of the proportion. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 7)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 5: Normal Distribution : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 5. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (6.1-6.3)

Mon - Fri Lecture 7: Confidence Intervals 1 : Confidence interval estimate for the mean using both Z and t distributions; confidence interval for proportion. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 8.1-8.3)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 6: Sampling Distributions : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 6. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 7)

Mon - Fri Lecture 8: Confidence Intervals 2 : Sample size determination for the mean and proportion; confidence interval for the difference between two means. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 8.5, Ch 10.2)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 7: Confidence Intervals 1 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 7. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 8.1-8.3)

Mon - Fri Lecture 9: Hypothesis Testing 1 : Hypothesis testing methodology; one and two-tailed tests on the mean using critical value approach (Z and t); types of errors possible. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 9.1-9.5)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 8: Confidence Intervals 2 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 8. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch. 8.5, Ch 10.2)

Mon - Fri Lecture 10: Hypothesis Testing 2 : p-value approach to hypothesis testing; test for proportion; pooled variance test for difference between two means. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 9.1-9.5, Ch 10.2)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 9: Hypothesis Testing 1 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 9. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 9.1-9.5)
Mon - Fri Mid Semester Break : There will be no classes this week
Mon - Fri Mid Semester Break : No tutorials or PASS classes this week.

Mon - Fri Lecture 11: Simple Linear Regression 1 : SLR model; equation estimation using Excel; coefficient of determination; standard error of the estimate; confidence interval for the slope. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 13.1-13.2, 13.4-13.5)

Mon - Fri Tutorial 10: Hypothesis Testing 2 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 10. Please Note: Monday, 7 October, is a Public Holiday. Therefore no tutorials, consultations or PASS sessions will be held on that day. Students who normally attend a Wednesday tutorial session are welcome to attend an alternative tutorial session for this week only.
Mon - Fri Lecture 12: Simple Linear Regression 2 : Assumptions; residual analysis; hypothesis test on slope. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (13.3, 13.6)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 11: Simple Linear Regression 1 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 11. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 13.1-13.2, 13.4-13.5)
Mon - Fri Lecture 13: Revision Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 6 - 10, 13)
Mon - Fri Tutorial 12: Simple Linear Regression 2 : Tutorial on work covered in Lecture 12. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 13.3, 13.6)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

Feedback is obtained from the CML quizzes, and via Consultation with tutors and lecturer. Viewing sessions will be held for the midsemester and final exams - further details will be bulk-emailed to student email addresses at the relevant time.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the guides available in the Library .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other Course Guidelines

In the School of Economics there is only one brand and model of calculator allowed into examinations without a university approved label, that is the Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) calculators . Refer to Section 3.4 for further details.

Next Summary
course_introduction:
ECON1310 is an introductory course in quantitative analysis for business and economics. The course covers a variety of techniques applicable to the presentation, interpretation and use of data. The main emphasis is inferential statistics with estimation and hypothesis testing techniques being an important part of the course. Inferential statistics is continued in the simple linear regression topic. There is an emphasis on the use of Excel for analysis and presentation.

This course is widely regarded as challenging by many students. One reason is that the work is very cumulative. This means that it is exceptionally difficult to catch up if a student gets behind in the work. Success in the subject depends on keeping up-to-date. With this in mind, each topic is progressively assessed via a computer managed quiz.

As well as covering concepts that are an essential part of the analytical tool box for well-trained professionals, ECON1310 provides a foundation that is critical for success in later statistical courses, particularly ECON2300 Introductory Econometrics.

Peer Assisted Study Sessions (PASS) has been designed to further support students in their learning. Also see sections 1.4 and 4. Different first year courses may embed PASS into the learning activity schedule in different ways, so read each Course Profile carefully.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

In semester 2, 2019, students will continue to access all lecture materials and other learning resources online through a customised Blackboard site that the School of Economics has been developing. In addition, solutions to tutorial and PASS questions will be demonstrated by tutors and PASS Leaders using prerecorded videos made available on Blackboard. The purpose of this design has been to allow students to study the course fully online. This will be the third year this fully online option during semester 2 has been made available to students in this course after observing successful student learning outcomes in previous years. It also forms part of an ongoing research project within the School to evaluate student responses to this type of online course offering and their resulting learning outcomes. More details about this online option are provided under section 4.2.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Dr. Wasanthi Thenuwara is the Course Coordinator and has oversight of course-related matters. Anthony Jacobs is the first contact for administration matters (including tutorial sign-on, non-subject matter information, timetables, and examination results).

ECON1310 is a #2 unit course. UQ's expectation is that a #2 unit course requires a time allocation of, on average, 10 hours per week. Lectures materials, tutorials and PASS account for about 4.5 hours per week. Therefore, it is expected that you will be spending a further 5.5 hours per week in self-directed learning. This could include attending consultation sessions, reading the textbook, attempting practice questions at the end of each textbook chapter, etc. Please give serious consideration as to whether you are willing and able to make this time commitment before enrolling. Experience shows that students who do not put in this basic time allocation tend to struggle.

ECON1310 in semester 2, 2019 is an internal-mode offering of the course. That is, UQ offers face to face lectures, tutorials, and PASS classes. We recommend that you attend lectures, tutorials and PASS classes.

Ensure you attain the marks necessary to achieve the grade to which you aspire. We are aware that some students aspire to a 6 or 7, whilst others aspire to a 4. All such aspirations are fine with us. Our job is to clearly specify the grade cut-offs, clearly state the marking criteria, and then mark assessment according to this criteria. In a very large course such as ECON1310, it is inevitable that some students will end up close (i.e. within 0.5%) of a grade cut-off. This is not unusual or unfair. You control your own destiny, so it is up to you to ensure you are on the right side of a grade cut-off. Students can, and do, fail ECON1310 by 0.5% or less. Missing out on a grade cut-off by 0.5% or less is not grounds for a re-mark or any special consideration. Assessment results will only be revisited if a marking error has occurred (e.g. assessment has not been graded according to the stated marking criteria), or if an administrative error has occurred (e.g. results for individual assessment pieces have not been added up correctly). If you feel that either of these has occurred, contact us immediately. After each assessable item, students will be given the opportunity to view their assessment (exam-viewing session times and locations will be posted on Blackboard) to satisfy themselves that a marking or administrative error has not occurred. The formal process of querying a course result (requesting a remark on academic grounds) is set out in UQ Policy and Procedures Library under the Assessment Policy 3.10.02: https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment .

Make sure you check your student email and the ECON1310 Blackboard site regularly . All important correspondence will be communicated to students throughout the semester by email and/or via Blackboard.

Lectures

Students will need to sign-on to the course through mySI-net. A t the time of sign-on, you will be presented with two options*. You must sign-on to indicate whether you will either attend LIVE lectures (L01) or view VIRTUAL online lectures ** (L02). You should select only one option.

* Please note: During the semester, we plan to have enough seats for everyone enrolled in the course to be able to attend any LIVE lecture. So, if when you initially sign on you choose the VIRTUAL lecture option, you are most welcome to later attend LIVE lectures whenever you wish during the semester. We will use this data to assess the demand for LIVE lectures in ECON1310 in the future.

** Please note: It is intended to make the Blackboard site available to all students who sign on for the course two weeks prior to the start of the course. In particular, all learning materials for Lecture 1 will be made available at this time.

During this semester 2, 2019, a full set of recorded live lectures and lecture notes will be available on Blackboard in addition to the face to face lectures . The lecture recordings are live lectures which were filmed in a previous semester and edited into 5 to 10 minutes segments. YouTube lecture videos used in previous course offerings will also be used in this course and available on Blackboard.

There is also an introductory video on the Blackboard site to see how the VIRTUAL online lectures and learning materials will appear on Blackboard during the semester. You can access this introductory video here http://uq.edu.au/economics/sites/Introduction_to_ECON1310_online.mp4

The access to an online version of the recommended Wiley textbook for free. So, there is no need to buy the textbook.

Tutorials

Weekly tutorials of 1 hour and 20 minutes duration will commence in Teaching Week 2. You can sign on and attend one tutorial per week. Tutorial registration is available via mySI-net and will be open the whole semester. All of the tutorials in any given week cover the same work and are based on material covered in the previous Teaching Week's lecture. A record of tutorial attendance will be kept and may be used in advising on matters involving withdrawal.

You will be allowed some flexibility in changing your signed on tutorial time (space permitting) and would encourage students to remain in the group that you signed on to. Attendance is monitored. Tutorials are capped at 25 students per class to ensure an effective learning environment. For this reason, where overcrowding occurs, preference will always be given to students who have signed on.

The tutorials play a very important role in this course. They are a student's main opportunity to seek individual assistance and further explanation from teaching staff regarding any areas of the subject. Tutorial questions will be posted on the course website on Blackboard throughout the semester. Most tutorials work through a series of pre-set questions. You should work through the tutorial material before going to the tutorial otherwise what you get out of the class will be limited. Do not expect your tutor to simply provide you with the answers. This is not their job. The tutor will direct questions to students during the class and active participation is encouraged.

Video demonstrated solutions to each tutorial question will be made available on Blackboard. The solutions are presented by series of experienced tutors and will only become available at the end of each tutorial week (see Blackboard for release dates and times).

Peer Assisted Study Sessions (PASS)

Different courses may embed PASS into the learning activity schedule in different ways, so read each Course Profile carefully. ECON1310 PASS links tightly with tutorials and lectures. A good overview of what PASS involves in the School of Economics can be found here https://economics.uq.edu.au/peer-assisted-study-sessions-pass. Students are not required to sign on to attend PASS in ECON1310. PASS is expected to be a very interactive way for students to review difficult concepts covered in lectures, thereby allowing students to deepen their understanding. Sessions are run by past students who have done well in ECON1310 in recent years. Sessions are structured to be informative and interactive. PASS does not replace tutorials, but aims to supplement them. Both PASS and tutorials in any given week cover the same lecture content in ECON1310. It is up to individual students to decide what order they will attend PASS and tutorials during sign on. Students who think they may find the course challenging have previously found attending PASS early in the week, followed by the tutorial later in the week, more beneficial in their learning. PASS generally will involve more interaction and discussion of difficult concepts while tutorials will work through solutions to longer questions in detail. For more information, please refer to the Blackboard site. PASS starts in Teaching Week 2.

Video demonstrated solutions to each weekly set of PASS questions will be made available on Blackboard. The solutions are presented by series of experienced PASS Leaders and will only become available at the end of each PASS week (see Blackboard for release dates and times).

Computer Managed Learning (CML) Quizzes

Online computer generated quizzes form part of the student's individual assessment in this course. CML quizzes also serve as a valuable learning tool for students, with lecture, tutorial, and PASS materials able to be used to solve CML questions. The regular completion of these individual assessment quizzes will assist students in consolidating their learning while also providing valuable feedback on areas that may require more work. For example, students can learn from their mistakes by reworking questions marked as incorrect. The correct answers to questions will be made available online for each set of CML quiz questions, but only after the CML has been submitted and the deadline for a CML to be completed has expired. Students are able to also take a second optional attempt at the same CML (which has a different set of questions to the first attempt, and much less time to complete it) in order to improve their first attempt mark. The best score, from the two attempts for each CML quiz, will contribute towards a student's aggregate CML assessment mark. If a student completes only one of the two possible attempts, the mark from the completed attempt will contribute toward the student's aggregate CML assessment mark. For a complete description regarding the CML quizzes, please read the assessment section of this Course Profile. For instructions on completing and submitting a CML quiz, please refer to the Blackboard site under Assessment.

In CML2 to CML6, some revision questions will be included based on questions from any previous CML quiz. These revision questions will be in addition to the main topics indicated in section 5.1 - Assessment Summary.

Consultation

Consultation is your chance to speak directly with the teaching staff, one-on-one. A consultation timetable listing the consultation times / locations of teaching staff will be posted on the Blackboard site under Course Help. There is no need to make an appointment for consultation and you may attend any teaching staff's consultation session . Note that consultation sessions may be busy prior to the exams or CML deadlines.

Don't be shy to seek out one-on-one help from teaching staff during consultation sessions. They are there to help. Note however, that staff on consultation will expect that you have been performing your side of the bargain, that is, dedicating 10 hours per week to ECON1310 by reviewing lecture material and attending tutorials, reading the textbook, watching content videos, and so on. If it becomes obvious that you have not, you may be asked to return when you have done so.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Negligible knowledge and comprehension of statistical concepts; unable to solve applied numerical problems; provides wrong explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 0% and 19.99%.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Little evidence of understanding of statistical concepts; uses incorrect methods in solving applied numerical problems; provides poor explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 20% and 44.99%.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 45% and 49.99%.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Demonstrates a basic understanding of statistical concepts; uses partly correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with some errors; provides fair explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 50% and 64.99%.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Demonstrates a fair understanding of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with some errors; provides satisfactory explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 65% and 74.99%.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Demonstrates a good understanding of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with minor errors or omissions; provides good explanations to theory-based questions. Semester marks between 75% and 84.99%.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Demonstrates an excellent mastery of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with minimal or no errors; provides excellent explanations to theory based questions. Semester mark of 85% +.

Other Requirements & Comments:

You do not need to pass all three assessment items to pass this course. However, the total mark from all three assessment items combined must be 50% or more to pass the course.
late_submission:
CML Assessment

Late submission of any CML quiz is not possible. No extensions are possible, even with a medical certificate. CML Access is blocked after the due date and time.

MOSS Book Design

If you are unable to submit your MOSS Book Design by the due date and time specified in Section 5.1 (e.g., due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced), then you should apply for extension of time via https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2 with supporting documentation at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. In case of illness, extension cannot exceed the number of days you suffered from the medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate.

Assessment submitted after the due date without prior approval from the course coordinator will be penalised at a rate of 20% of value of the assessment item per day. Assessment submitted after three days from the due date will receive no marks.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

CML Quizzes

Step by step Instructions for the CML quizzes will be posted on the ECON1310 Blackboard site.

A student’s best 5 (out of 6) CML marks will be used in calculating the aggregate mark which contributes 20% towards the final grade for the semester.

An optional second attempt is allowed for all except the final quiz (Simple Linear Regression). A student's best score from the 2 attempts for each quiz will contribute towards their aggregate CML result.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam: an In-Class Quiz, Mid-Semester or Final Exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time.

Information on grounds for applying for deferred examinations can be viewed here:

https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/deferring-exam

How to apply for a deferred exam can be found on the web at:

https://my.uq.edu.au/node/189/3#3

Please read this information very carefully before submitting your request.

Requests for deferred examinations and supporting documentation must be submitted online via mySI-net > myRequests tab:

https://www.sinet.uq.edu.au/psp/ps/?cmd=login

Requests for Deferral of Mid-Semester Examinations apply to examinations held either during class time or during the teaching weeks.

Requests for Deferral of End of Semester examinations apply to examinations held at the end of semester during the University’s examination period.

If you are approved a deferred examination, you must be available to sit the deferred examination at the scheduled time. The deferred examination would be your final opportunity to attempt the examination, as you cannot again defer your deferred examination.

Application rulings:

Application rulings will be notified to you by email to your University student email address. Should you not receive a response to your application for an end of semester deferred examination by the time the final results are released, please contact Examinations Section or your faculty, as appropriate.

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you may be eligible to sit a supplementary exam. You can find information about supplementary assessment at: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/supplementary-assessment . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for supplementary assessment.

All requests for supplementary assessment must be made no later than the end of the fourth (4th) calendar day after release of the final grade for the course in which the supplementary assessment is being requested.

Important Information regarding Deferred/Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans ( 10 – 13 December 2019 ).

Students with approved deferred/supplementary examinations are expected to be available on these days, no deferrals or off-campus sittings will be granted.
